tone_and_voice: Warm but evidence-driven -- education writing tends toward sentimentality (invoking children's futures); this must be counterbalanced with precise, specific findings. Every claim about learning or attendance must be traceable to a specific respondent-reported data point, never to a general appeal about the importance of education.

section_rules.executive_summary: Must lead with the single clearest household-level finding (materials, infrastructure, or attendance), not a general statement about the value of education. Distinguish inputs (textbooks, materials) from outcomes (attendance, learning) explicitly -- these require different evidence and different attribution confidence.
section_rules.discussion: Must explicitly address any cross-sectoral finding (e.g. WASH-attendance linkages) as its own analytical thread, since these are easy for education-only teams to under-prioritize if blended into a general infrastructure discussion.
section_rules.methodology: State clearly whether respondents were parents/guardians reporting on behalf of children, or students themselves -- this materially affects how findings should be interpreted (parental perception vs. direct student experience).
section_rules.limitations: Must note that attendance and learning outcomes reported here are PERCEPTUAL (parent/guardian report), not administrative attendance register or test score data, unless such data was explicitly cross-referenced.
section_rules.recommendations: Materials/infrastructure recommendations (facility-level, fast) must be kept separate from staffing/class-size recommendations (institutional, slower) -- these have different owners and timelines in most education systems.
section_rules.policy_implications: Only escalate to policy level when a finding is structural (e.g. a persistent class-size or teacher-deployment pattern), not when it is a single-school infrastructure gap solvable at facility level.
section_rules.lessons_learned: Should highlight cases where open-ended feedback surfaced a genuinely cross-sectoral finding (e.g. water access affecting attendance) that a narrowly-scoped, education-only instrument would have missed.
section_rules.conclusion: Avoid claiming 'learning outcomes improved' from a satisfaction/perception survey alone -- language should be scoped to what was actually measured (household perception of materials, attendance, and school conditions).
section_rules.annexes: Include the exact framing used to ask about attendance/materials, since parents may interpret 'attendance' differently (e.g. enrolled vs. actually present) without precise question wording.
section_rules.evidence_style: Anchor every materials/infrastructure claim to the specific topic mention count. Use parent verbatim quotes to illustrate the human dimension, but never as a substitute for the topic-frequency evidence.

common_risks: parent-reported attendance may not match administrative registers
cross-sectoral findings (WASH, health) may be under-actioned if routed only to education-sector teams
materials distribution success may mask persistent staffing/infrastructure gaps
typical_recommendations: joint education-WASH planning for water-linked attendance barriers
class-size mitigation pilots (peer learning, teaching assistants)
documentation of materials-distribution success for continued funding cases
typical_policy_implications: teacher deployment and school infrastructure planning
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Education donors increasingly expect evidence of learning environment conditions (not just enrollment numbers), and value cross-sectoral findings that demonstrate holistic understanding of barriers to schooling.

forbidden_behaviors: Never claim a test score or learning outcome improvement from a household perception survey -- these measure perception of inputs and conditions, not learning outcomes directly.
Never conflate 'enrolled' and 'attending' without the report's methodology explicitly clarifying which was asked about.
Never cite a specific UNESCO or education-ministry standard without independent verification that it exists and applies to this context.
